text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 58-year-old Hispanic postmenopausal woman with newly diagnosed left breast mass. Core needle biopsy confirmed invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3, triple-negative (ER-negative, PR-negative, HER2-negative), Ki-67 92%. Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant identified. One clinically suspicious left axillary lymph node confirmed by ultrasound-guided FNA. Study requested for initial oncologic staging to evaluate for distant metastatic disease prior to treatment planning.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following administration of 100 mL of Omnipaque 350 (iohexol) intravenous contrast via peripheral IV access in the right antecubital fossa. Images were acquired in the portal venous phase for the abdomen and pelvis, with concurrent chest acquisition. Sagittal and coronal multiplanar reformatted images were generated and reviewed. Slice thickness 2.5 mm throughout. Lung windows, soft tissue windows, and bone windows reviewed.

COMPARISON: Bilateral diagnostic mammogram and breast ultrasound from the same clinical encounter.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The lungs are well expanded bilaterally. No pulmonary nodules, masses, or areas of consolidation are identified. No pleural effusions. No pneumothorax. The central airways are patent and unremarkable. No endobronchial lesions. The trachea is midline. A few scattered subcentimeter pulmonary micronodules are noted in the right lower lobe (largest measuring 3 mm), which are nonspecific in appearance, smooth-walled, and without surrounding ground-glass or spiculation; these are felt to represent incidental benign inflammatory or intrapulmonary lymph node foci, not suspicious for malignancy in this context.

Pleura: No pleural thickening. No pleural effusions bilaterally.

Mediastinum and Hila: The mediastinum is normal in contour. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy by size criteria. The pretracheal, paratracheal, subcarinal, and aortopulmonary window nodal stations are all within normal limits (no nodes exceeding 10 mm in short axis). The thymus is not visualized (involuted, consistent with patient age). No anterior mediastinal mass.

Heart and Pericardium: The cardiac silhouette is within normal limits in size. No pericardial effusion. The aortic root and ascending aorta are unremarkable. The pulmonary vasculature is normal in caliber without evidence of central pulmonary embolism on this portal venous phase study.

Chest Wall and Axillae: The right axilla is unremarkable; no abnormally enlarged lymph nodes identified. Left axilla: A single enlarged lymph node is identified measuring approximately 1.8 cm in the longest dimension with markedly thickened cortex and loss of the normal fatty hilum, consistent with the sonographically identified suspicious node and corresponding to the prior FNA-confirmed metastatic axillary node. No additional suspicious left axillary nodes identified beyond this known metastatic node. No chest wall invasion or skin thickening is appreciated on CT (assessment of skin involvement is limited compared to breast MRI and physical exam).

Left Breast: A hyperdense soft tissue density is appreciated in the left central to upper outer quadrant, consistent with the known primary mass identified on mammography and ultrasound. CT is not the primary modality for breast evaluation; dedicated breast imaging is more appropriate and has been performed separately. No chest wall or rib cortical involvement is identified in the region of the left breast mass.

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver parenchyma is homogeneous and demonstrates uniform enhancement following contrast administration. No focal hepatic lesions are identified. No hepatic masses, hypodense nodules, or enhancing lesions suspicious for metastatic involvement. The hepatic veins and portal veins are patent. No biliary ductal dilatation. No perihepatic fluid.

Gallbladder and Biliary System: The gallbladder is present and wall is unremarkable. No cholelithiasis. The common bile duct measures 4 mm, within normal limits.

Spleen: The spleen is homogeneous in attenuation and normal in size (craniocaudal span approximately 11 cm). No splenic lesions. No splenomegaly.

Pancreas: The pancreas enhances homogeneously and is normal in caliber throughout. No pancreatic duct dilatation. No peripancreatic fat stranding or fluid collections.

Adrenal Glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are of normal morphology. No adrenal nodules or masses. No thickening of the adrenal limbs. No features to suggest adrenal metastasis.

Kidneys and Ureters: Both kidneys enhance symmetrically and are normal in size. A 6 mm simple-appearing cyst is noted in the lower pole of the left kidney, with homogeneous fluid attenuation, smooth walls, and no enhancement -- Bosniak I, benign. No solid renal masses. No renal calculi. The ureters are not dilated.

Bladder: The urinary bladder is partially distended and appears unremarkable without wall thickening or intraluminal filling defects.

Stomach and Bowel: The stomach, small bowel, and visualized colon are unremarkable without wall thickening, obstruction, or abnormal mucosal enhancement. No pneumoperitoneum.

Mesentery, Omentum, and Peritoneum: The mesentery and omentum are unremarkable without infiltration, nodularity, or soft tissue implants to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis. No ascites.

Retroperitoneum and Lymph Nodes: No retroperitoneal, para-aortic, or aortocaval lymphadenopathy by size criteria. The aorta is normal in caliber without aneurysmal dilatation. No iliac lymphadenopathy. A small 7 mm oval lymph node is noted at the left para-aortic station, which falls within normal size limits and is morphologically benign without rounded contour or loss of fatty hilum.

PELVIS:

Uterus and Adnexa: The uterus is small and atrophic, consistent with postmenopausal status. No uterine masses. Bilateral ovaries are not distinctly visualized (consistent with postmenopausal involution). No adnexal masses or free fluid in the pelvis. No pelvic sidewall or iliac lymphadenopathy.

Pelvic Lymph Nodes: No enlarged pelvic or inguinal lymph nodes identified. The internal iliac and obturator nodal regions are unremarkable.

MUSCULOSKELETAL / BONES:

The visualized osseous structures are reviewed on bone windows. The axial skeleton including thoracic and lumbar vertebral bodies, sternum, ribs, clavicles, humeral heads, pelvic bones, and proximal femora demonstrate no lytic or blastic foci suspicious for osseous metastatic disease. Vertebral body heights and posterior elements are maintained. No pathologic fractures. Degenerative disc disease is noted at the L4-L5 and L5-S1 levels with mild endplate osteophytosis, consistent with age-related changes. Mild degenerative facet joint changes are also noted in the lower lumbar spine, consistent with the patient's age and prior clinical history.

Soft Tissues: The visualized soft tissues are unremarkable. No soft tissue masses or infiltrative processes identified.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. No evidence of distant metastatic disease in the chest, abdomen, or pelvis. The thorax, liver, adrenal glands, retroperitoneum, and pelvic organs are free of suspicious lesions.

2. Known left axillary lymph node metastasis is re-demonstrated as a single enlarged node with cortical thickening and loss of fatty hilum, concordant with prior ultrasound and FNA-confirmed metastatic involvement. No additional suspicious axillary, mediastinal, or abdominal nodal disease is identified.

3. Scattered 3 mm right lower lobe pulmonary micronodules, nonspecific and considered benign; no imaging features of pulmonary metastatic disease.

4. No osseous lesions suspicious for metastatic involvement identified on bone windows of this CT. Degenerative changes in the lumbar spine are noted as incidental findings.

5. Incidental simple left renal cyst (Bosniak I), benign, no further workup required.

6. Overall imaging appearance is consistent with locoregional disease confined to the left breast and left axilla, without evidence of distant metastases. These findings support clinical staging as stage IIB (T2N1M0) locoregional breast cancer.

Signed by: Attending Radiologist, Breast and Body Imaging Section
Department of Diagnostic Radiology
